Example problem: 0.1 kg ball, 10 m/s at 30 degrees, air resistance constant 0.01 kg/m. Calculated max height: 1.2743 m, landing distance: 4.4157 m. Used equations with gravity, air resistance proportional to velocity squared.
Key equations: Range = v² sin(2θ)/g; Time of flight = 2v sin(θ)/g; Max height = v² sin²(θ)/(2g); Velocity components: vx = v₀ cos(θ), vy = v₀ sin(θ) - gt.

Identify air resistance: problem mentions velocity, proportionality constant, projectile mass/density, problem asks to account for air resistance, real-world context likely has air resistance. More complex calculations, may need numerical methods.

Ocean temperatures/weather affect marine life: species migrate to cooler waters as ocean warms; changes in sea ice, currents, coastal upwelling impact marine life; species depending on sea ice (polar bears, penguins, seals) struggle; reduced dissolved oxygen creates 'dead zones'.
Climate change impacts: warming oceans shift entire ecosystems, disrupts predator-prey relationships, affects food chains; dead zones in coastal waters; imbalances in nutrient cycling.
Mitigation strategies: reduce carbon footprint, protect marine ecosystems/MPAs, reduce pollution/plastic, educate people, support research/conservation efforts.

User motivated to reduce carbon footprint. Individual actions: reduce energy/water use, reduce transportation emissions, reduce food waste/use local produce, reduce plastic, switch to renewable energy.

'Childhood's End' (1953): Alien 'Overlords' arrive in ships above major cities mid-20th century. Initially seem benevolent, guide humanity toward peace/prosperity. Protagonist Jan Rodricks questions motives. Explores first contact, humanity's destiny, freedom vs security, evolution/transcendence.

'2001: A Space Odyssey' themes: human evolution, technology/AI relationship, existentialism, unknown/unknowable. Similarities to 'Childhood's End': explore next human evolution step, philosophical/intellectual tone, slow-burning contemplative pacing. Differences: optimistic vs enigmatic tone, traditional vs experimental narrative.

User is writing fictional character Val Renberg: autistic female spacer/engineer, brusque and blunt with big words and engineering metaphors, mindset like a 1950s male pilot/engineer, one of her Co-Op's most brilliant minds.

Val is single-mindedly devoted to Co-Modularism: a design/accessibility philosophy emphasizing circular economy, post-scarcity resource management, and inclusion of diverse sentient species in deep-space cooperative living.
Val regards the Co-Op as her mother and father; it cared for and educated her when her parents weren't up to the job, making her fiercely loyal to it.
Val has worked beside and intimately loved aliens; her late husband R'kesh was Novayan. Through him she adopted Novayan spirituality, which holds all things, including ships, have a spirit.

Val self-regulates with a water-filled crash couch named "Sputnik" (after the first satellite), like a tightening waterbed; she designed an upgrade adding vibration, sound, and temperature regulation as a personal sensory-deprivation tank.
Val has objectum feelings for her ship, experiencing it as a living being others depend on; the crash couch connects her intimately to the ship's spirit.
At user's request, assistant role-played as Val, speaking as a stern elder spacer explaining CoModularism principles to a young apprentice.

Val: autistic spacer/engineer, co-op most brilliant minds, accomplished. Uses engineering metaphors, brusque language, big words. 1950s male pilot/engineer mindset. Worked with aliens, loved intimately. Co-Op was mother/father (educated/cared when parents didn't).

Val passionately devoted to Co-Modularism: design/accessibility philosophy enabling wide-range needs, promoting sustainability/resource efficiency. Circular economy/post-scarcity principles. Respects diversity of sentient beings. Collaborative, not just for humanity.

Val autistic in deep space: routine/predictability comforting; isolation challenging without sensory regulation or social interaction. Autism gives unique problem-solving perspective, detail-oriented focus essential for complex systems/life support. Not everyone understanding; some see differences as weakness.

Val uses water-filled crash couch named 'Sputnik' for sensory regulation. Like tightening waterbed, weightlessness, pressure against body. Nod to early space exploration. Designed upgrades with vibration, sound, temperature regulation for enhanced self-regulation.

Val sees ship as living being on whom others depend. Crash couch connection extends to ship itself. Autistic attachment to objects/routines. Novayan late husband r'kesh influenced spirituality: Novayans believe all things have spirit/essence. Ship has spirit/essence.

Craving warmth/coziness winter: evolutionary roots (seek warmth/shelter for survival); cold stress response (cortisol/adrenaline trigger discomfort/anxiety, seeking warmth mitigates); social/emotional connections (indoor time, warmth substitutes isolation); nostalgia/sentimentality (childhood memories); hormonal changes (serotonin/dopamine/melatonin affected).

Yellowstone road trip: 1,400 miles round trip, 28 mpg highway, 300 miles/day, $2.75/gallon. Calculation: 50 gallons needed, ~$137.50 fuel cost.

Yellowstone attractions: Old Faithful (geothermal geyser), Grand Prismatic Spring (largest US hot spring), Upper/Lower Yellowstone Falls, Mammoth Hot Springs, wildlife viewing (grizzly bears, wolves, bison, elk, moose).
Yellowstone routes: Grand Loop Road (142 miles through main attractions), Norris Geyser Basin (geothermal features), Lamar Valley (wildlife/scenic vistas/Lamar River).

Norris Geyser Basin attractions: Steamboat Geyser (tallest active, unpredictable), Echinus Geyser (frequent eruptions), Emerald Pool (vibrant green hot spring). Trails: 1.5-mile Norris loop, 0.5-mile Porcelain, 1.2-mile Back Basin. Tips: take time, respect fragile environment, expect crowds (visit early/late to avoid).
Gothic literature setting/atmosphere: gloomy, eerie, creepy, evoking dread/foreboding. Heavy, oppressive atmosphere with darkness/shadows/unknown. Creates unease/tension essential to genre.

Gothic masters: Edgar Allan Poe ('Fall of House of Usher', 'Tell-Tale Heart' set in gloomy decay), Mary Shelley ('Frankenstein' Gothic castle), Bram Stoker ('Dracula' decaying mansion), Charlotte Bronte ('Jane Eyre' Thornfield Hall/moors).

Edgar Allan Poe: dark/twisted tales, psychological depth, masterful language/poetic style. Most influential gothic writer. Influenced by life tragedies (father death, mother early death, foster father conflict).

'Black Cat': reflects Poe's cat love before alcoholism, guilt over mistreatment. 'Fall of House of Usher': madness/decay drawn from family deaths/addiction/depression. Personal experiences informed writing, though often fantastical/exaggerated.

Autobiographical literature: Ernest Hemingway ('Sun Also Rises' Paris expat, 'Farewell to Arms' WWI), James Joyce ('Portrait of Artist' based on life), Virginia Woolf ('To Lighthouse' childhood summers, 'Mrs. Dalloway' partly autobiographical), Louisa May Alcott ('Little Women' family/Jo March as self).

20th-century photography innovations: color photography (19th experimentation, 20th widespread adoption), 35mm film (1920s, more pictures/skills), digital cameras (late 20th revolution), sophisticated lenses (detailed intimate shots), advanced printing (high-quality images).

Future photography innovations: AI/machine learning software, augmented/virtual reality immersion, hybrid photography (combining art forms), light field photography (refocusing/perspective changes post-capture).
Drone photography will grow: offers unique aerial perspective. Becoming affordable/accessible. Advancing features: 360-degree cameras, improved image quality/zoom, longer battery life.

White balsamic: lighter color/flavor, white grape must cooked low-temp to avoid caramelization, shorter aging in stainless/light-wood barrels, light/fruity flavor. Dark balsamic: caramelized grape must, aged in dark-wood barrels, thick/syrupy consistency, sweet/tangy complex flavor.

White balsamic: delicate dishes, salads, dressings, pairs with fruits/vegetables/seafood, grilled chicken/fish, roasted veggies, fruit salads. Dark balsamic: glazes/sauces, meats, hearty veggies, marinades, drizzles over roasted items.

New balsamic vinaigrette: 1/4 cup balsamic, 2 tbsp honey, 1 tbsp Dijon mustard, garlic/onion powder, dried basil/thyme, salt/pepper, 1/2 cup olive oil. Whisk vinegar/honey/mustard/spices, slowly add oil to emulsify. Adjust seasoning.

Creative balsamic uses: glazed roasted veggies (carrots, beets, Brussels sprouts), balsamic reduction (simmer until syrupy), marinated meats (balsamic/oil/garlic/rosemary), cocktails (balsamic/bourbon/honey/lemon), fruit skewers (brush glaze before grilling).
